content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies with a color in the name

**Score:** 55
**Body:** hey all,

super simple suggestion but i realized that the last two movies i have watched were White Sands(1992) and Black Rain(1989) so thought it would be fun to keep this trend going by completing a movie marathon with different colors in their names.

any genre can do(besides horror) and sub colors such as carolina blue are welcomed too.

thanks y'all!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Color Purple (1985)
2. Three Colors Trilogy - Krzysztof Kieślowski
3. Fried Green Tomatoes
4. Blue Velvet

Perfect Blue
5. The Hunt for RED october 

BLUE is the warmest color 

The GREEN Knight 

Soldier of ORANGE 

Fifty Shades of GREY 

Jackie BROWN 

PURPLE Rain 

YELLOW Submarine 

The PINK Panther
  - BLACK Dynamite
6. Turning red.
7. A Clockwork Orange

Blue Ruin
8. My Blue Heaven
9. The Green Mile

## 2. Perfume: The Story Of A Murder (2006) (Thriller/Fantasy)

**Score:** 41
**Body:** I was recently reminded of this, after having not seen it since it was released for sale. 

I remember being utterly enthralled, and quite satisfied by the premise, as well as the execution. There’s something bizarrely romantic about it, despite the creepy elements. Ben Whishaw was believable and charming. The supporting cast all deliver top-tier performances. 

Currently looking forward to a rewatch in the near future, I thought It would be interesting to bring this up, and see what your thoughts on it are, and how many of you it resonated with. More than that, I’m curious how many have never even heard of it. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I was obsessed enough with this to follow its production until release. I thought it was practically made in a lab for me because I *adore* the book, AND they cast Alan Rickman!? Heaven! 


As an adaptation, it leaves something to be desired, but as an atmospheric film it hits almost all of the right vibes. 


Although, it definitely commits the sin of making what was meant to be a repulsive character into an "Oh no, he's hot!" Villain 


https://tvtropes.org/pmwiki/pmwiki.php/Main/HollywoodHomely


Edit to say: seriously, I can't recommend the book enough. If you loved the themes and the scent obsession elements from the film, they're there in excess on the page. And it's so fun to see the sense of smell translated into language. 
  - TIL! I wasn’t aware that this an adaptation, though I don’t exactly find it surprising. Moreso, simply unaware. 

I assume you didn’t find the differences outright egregious? (Aside from the attractive lead actor)
2. Is that set in pre-revolutionary France?

I think I read the book and it’s definitely an unusual plot.
  - It is a book by Patrick Suskind. Kurt Cobain was obsessed with it, and it inspired the song “Scentless Apprentice”
3. One of those rare movies that doesn't betray the novel. Ben Whitshaw is excellent. gorgeously filmed. hard to believe it's the same director who did Run Lola Run!
  - There’s another title that was once on my radar, since slipped away. I’ll have to put that on my watchlist
4. My second favourite film of all time
5. OP you might like the tv serial on Netflix with the same name. It's German so please watch with subtitles. Also it's modern day.
6. Dude yes! That movie is so underrated, barely anyone I know has seen it. The whole scent obsession thing was genuinely unsettling but weirdly captivating at the same time. Ben Whishaw nailed that creepy innocent vibe perfectly
7. Yeah, a fave of mine. I still listen to the soundtrack every now and then.

## 3. train movies

**Score:** 30
**Body:** looking for movies that mostly take place on a train. so far, we've watched Unstoppable, Snowpiercer, and Bullet Train. Train to Busan is next on our list!!

edit: i've seen murder on the orient express (2017), and i didn't love it because i liked the book way more. i'll have to revisit it as a Train Movie instead of a movie based on a mystery novel lol

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Source Code (2011)
2. Runaway Train (1985)
3. Murder on the Orient Express (2017)
  - I am here as a representative of the 1974 version. 


But I will say that the David Suchet 2010 Poirot episode reigns supreme, since it is a part of a legacy series. (It is 90 minutes, so it's basically a made-for-TV movie and I think that counts.)
4. The Darjeeling Limited
5. Silver Streak
6. Polar Express.
7. Under Siege 2: Dark Territory (1995)
8. Strangers on a Train
9. The Taking of pelham 123 (2009)

## 4. I recommend Easy Rider

**Score:** 22
**Body:** I recommend Easy Rider

Easy Rider is in my opinion the greatest motorcycles/biker/drug movie ever. The soundtrack is one of the the best movie soundtracks ever. The music just goes with the movie so well. I have watched this over and over and I can wait to show it to my daughter. She’s only 7 months old. My grandpa and his brother introduced me to this movie and I finally got a dvd to watch it. Jack Nicholson’s greatest movie. Peter Fonda is an amazing actor.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "A man went looking for America. And couldn't find it anywhere . ."

One of the best taglines ever written for a movie.
2. Finally caught it for the first time a couple years back and that scene by the campfire where Jack is talking about freedom, man.. it just leveled me. What a picture.
  - Yes it did me too man. I always fire one up as soon as they do. Supposedly real weed was used in the movie
3. i think this undersells it a lot. easy rider is those things for sure, but it’s also a seminal film about what hunter s thompson describes as the high tide mark of the 60s in fear and loathing in las vegas.

i don’t think anyone gatekeeps easy rider but i do think it gets overlooked for the totality of what it is outside of enthusiasts of the era. dennis hopper had a lot of wild acting roles, but talking to him about photography would have been the real joy. his apocalypse now character really was the perfect image of him, i think.
4. If you have the disc, Dennis Hopper’s commentary is nice.
  - I have the remaster dvd
  - I have the vinyl pressing
5. When you get to the right place, with the right people, quarter this.
6. I saw this in the theater when it came out. I've tried watching a streaming version of it but the quality is horrible.
7. One of my favorite movies.  
First it is a decent biker movie of three hippies driving Harley Davidson's but in my opinion this movie has a deeper and far more important message: The bigotry about your own freedom versus the freedom of others (especially in the second half o the movie). To gain their own freedom they continually restrict the freedom of the three bikers >!until they kill Hanson and later the pick-up driver commit a double homicide and get away with it!<.

Nice suggestion! 👍

## 5. Just finished watching “The Stanford Prison experiment” (2015)

**Score:** 20
**Body:** If you’ve never heard about the experiment, do not google it. Just go in it blind and watch from beginning to end. Then google the experiment if you’d like.

Anyway this is the first movie I’ve watched in a long time that has really left me shocked. It’s one of those movies I’ll be thinking of years after.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Here are a handful of similar, highly sociological movies that I'll suggest:

* Hannah Arendt (2012)
* Die Welle (a.k.a. The Wave) (2008)
* Experimenter (a.k.a. The Stanley Milgram Story) (2015)

Other movies based on Philip Zimbardo's Stanford Prison experiment (the fist is the best, IMHO):

* Das Experiment (a.k.a. The Experiment) (2001)
* The Experiment (2010) - American remake of above German film

I can think of other sociological movies that might be of interest; let me know, if interested.
2. Don’t. The results were skewed by the director who would push and encouraged the guards for results. 

It’s been disproven as an unbiased study.

Edit: the original study director was my intent
  - They were taught to be cruel ffs
  - Things were a lot different in that era for sure with testing, rules and procedures but let’s say he did encourage them, what does that say about the guards? 

Or how easy it would be for someone with more authority to control those with less, simply for a paycheck? 

It’s just food for thought is all.
    - Very good points. The guards didn’t object
3. Check out the older one “Punishment Park” also blind.
4. You may also be interested in the Stanley Milgram biopic “Experimeter” (2015) which also documents his famous experiments in “Obedience to Authority”; “Compliance” (2012), which dramatizes a real-life application of these concepts in an ordinary workplace; and “Lesson Plan” (2010), a documentary about The Third Wave social experiment at a Palo Alto high school in 1967.
5. Now watch "Silo".
6. This was not a proud moment for America.
  - But maybe a necessary one?

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Bullet Train (2022) is incredible

**Score:** 1845
**Body:** I love this movie so much. It has so much awesome action, great jokes, amazing story telling and great characters. I mean come on, Russian samurai Michael Shannon? Crazy movie. I'm shocked that Lemon and Tangerine haven't gotten a spinoff yet. They were hilarious and made me emotional multiple times throughout the film. I've seen it 3 times now and enjoyed it just as much the 3rd time as I did the first 2. What do you guys think about this film and do you see a spinoff coming in the future?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It was great. I'd love to see more Lemon & Tangerine.
  - Lemon and Tangerine: Brothers in Bolivia
  - If you like reading the author has like 3 other books and they’re very good. Except for mantis which is kinda not as good
2. I heard someone compare it to a live action episode of Archer and that’s probably a fair description. Which is probably why I loved it.
  - That is actually a great parallel. I finally watched this the other night and absolutely loved it.
3. If you don’t enjoy this film, you’re just a diesel.
4. Yes 


I still don't understand why the reviews for this are so low. Like do y'all not like fun
  - Bunch of fucking diesels.
5. The easiest way to rate an action comedy is on rewatchability. And I’ll watch this any time it’s on.
6. Don’t call me bro!!!!!!!!!!

## 2. this cast might be the reason why GoldenEye is my favorite Bond movie

**Score:** 889
**Body:** (close with Casino Royale of course)

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Loved this one and the Nintendo 64 game !
  - Game was/is classic.
2. Minnie Driver as the warbling mistress
  - "Stahnd by yewer maaaaaaaan...."
3. Famke Jansen was a 5 alarm fire in this one
4. This is what one would call a proper reboot, even though Brosnan is the same Bond as his predecessors. Everything about GoldenEye was just fresh, and that's why it's my favorite Bond film.
  - It’s my first Bond film and Onatopp awoke something in me as a little boy
5. Man I had no idea Jean grey, nightcrawler, and boromir/ned stark were all in goldeneye
  - Don’t forget Hagrid
    - Also the boyfriend from Mrs Doubtfire

## 3. As a South African, I just want to know if the non-South Africans understood the dialogue, slangs and deeper implications of District 9

**Score:** 642

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. One thing I didn’t pick up on is that the name “prawns” were a play on the Park-town Prawns. I thought they were comparing them to shrimp and I was like, yeah I can kinda see it.
  - Oh I still thought it was shrimp 
2. So the problem with not picking up on the deeper implications of something is I don't know ive not picked up on them
  - What I’m saying is there’s known knowns and known unknowns, but that *theres also* unknown unknowns! You know what I’m sayin??
    - Suddenly Rumsfeld.
3. Yes. It's not exactly subtle.
4. Can you provide any examples?
5. No man, the whole time none of us from the rest of the world had any idea of what was going on
6. Was this not a documentary?
7. I realized that if you say fuck off backwards it sounds like you're saying it in a South African accent, if that counts?

## 4. The Smashing Machine is not a movie. It’s a collection of scenes with no plot.

**Score:** 461
**Body:** DJ does some interesting things, and Emily Blunt is OK, but it felt like she lost a bet on the set of jungle cruise or something to do this movie. Pretty disappointed in the thing except for DJs acting, which shows some range, but not near the range many people think. I’m not sure if Kerr’s story just isn’t compelling enough, but this was a miss for me. I can confidently say I will never watch this again.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Mark Kerr is a tragic figure. This movie could have been great if it appealed to MMA fans who appreciated Kerr. But it doesn’t… it’s a bland Hollywood attempt at things.
  - The director butchered it. I saw him describe his thought process and he said he wanted it to be a positive story...

Brother the documentary is a work of art because it shows a world class athlete lose the battle to drugs and alcohol and end his career by choosing his addict girlfriend over his coach's advice. Just watch the doc if you haven't
    - Probably the Rock’s contract wouldn’t allow him to lose a fight against drugs onscreen

/j, I haven’t seen the movie
    - I viewed it as a poor dramatization of a really good doc that no one wanted.  I couldn't stop laughing at how stiff and big rock looked.  Hes a 50 year old roided-out bodybuilder who is too big to play a 30 yr star athlete.
2. Yeah I watched this and it wasn’t what I expected. It was essentially just a shitty marriage with some fights in the background. Or maybe that was the point? Idk it didn’t have any background or storyline lol just jumped in when he was already fighting and kinda went nowhere 🤷🏻‍♂️
3. In comparison to the documentary it was said to have been partly based on, very underwhelming movie.
4. A collection of scenes with no plot can be a movie.
5. Idk guys I liked it

Unremarkable plot about an unremarkable career, yet the movie and the story still stuck with me

There’s nothing wrong with being considered average, you can still make a wider impact than you initially realize. Ironically, I feel the same way about the film.
  - Yeah I felt it to be observational and more of a character study type of thing rather than a traditional 3 act structure
6. I'm a huge MMA fan, and I was so disappointed in this movie.   For starters, there are so many great MMA athletes with crazy life stories, why the hell did they pick Mark Kerr out of anyone? Ok, so he dealt with steroid abuse and marriage conflicts. Who didn't back then?

In general, I'm also not a fan of The Rock as an actor, but this time it's not even his fault. There is no plot, no excitement, nothing really.

## 5. Your opinion on Paul Dano as an actor

**Score:** 300
**Body:** Paul Dano with tarantino 

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Tarantino hating him is actually making him way more popular rn
  - On that note, does anyone actually agree with Tarantino on this? I thought he was phenomenal in “There Will Be Blood”.
    - Also Prisoners and little miss sunshine. Showed a lot of range
      - I thought he was perfect for Riddler.
    - I think Tarrantino sort of missed the nuance of TWBB and the juxtaposition of the two characters.  He's used to Samuri fights and two equally outwardly skilled characters as foes.   But, I think he might not understand the real psychological snake oil salesman power behind Evangelical Christians.

IMO Both characters Eli and Daniel were con artists manipulating people for money.   Eli used the church and God.   Daniel considered himself a God.   That's why I thought one of the powerful scenes in the movie is when Daniel has to submit himself to God in front of the whole church. Eli knows that Daniel is just pretending for the sake of money.  But he makes him do it anyway.  This is why Daniel hates him so much.  They both knew it was fake.  And they both knew the other one knew.

The fact that the brothers are played by Paul Dano who looks boyish and innocent is just as much a part of the ruse as Daniel looking wise and rugged and caring for a young boy.

I think people born into Christian families who have watched how religion has been used in such a fake but effective way, come away from the film with a much different and deeper understanding.   Someone who has never gone through this experience doesn't get the power play at all.    To them it just looks like a wolf against a sheep.  To us it looks like a wolf against a wolf in sheep's' clothing.
    - He was fantastic in TWBB and The Batman
2. He’s a helluva lot better than Tarantino as an actor
3. He's alright. He gets typecasting as a little weirdo a lot. I want him to branch out.
  - Yes I would like to see him in the role of a giant weirdo instead.
4. I mean he’s ok I liked him in little miss sunshine

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Crooklyn—One of Lee’s Best

**Score:** 25
**Body:** I’ve done a deep-dive of Lee’s filmography, and although there’s at least 5 phenomenal films, I do tend find a paradox in Lee’s work that, is at times, detrimental. The paradox is that some of his films tend to be incredibly real in their portrayal of certain issues, while also being highly melodramatic and cheesy. I think I noticed this most with Jungle Fever, which featured some absolute brutal depictions of the crack epidemic, which I have to think was progressive for the time, but simultaneously, the acting in the film feels melodramatic and superficial. 

But man, I think Crooklyn strikes the perfect balance. The acting seems authentic, and the film itself is genuine poignant. In addition, it has to be one of his most visually arresting films. Anyway, I feel like this film doesn’t get enough recognition in his catalogue. 

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. People forget that first and foremost Spike is a stylist. They think of him as the gritty Black drama guy. But he loves cinema. He's not polemical, people just perceive him that way because they don't understand the Black experience, which he doesn't portray especially radically. He's an artist and what he really loves to do is experiment. So there's a lot of hit and miss but when everything comes together it is some of the most audacious and innovative narrative cinema out there.
  - Yeah, with only a couple of films left to watch, I think this is a perfect description of him as a director.
2. Off topic, but your comment reminded me of Charles Dickens. Dickens would write a book that had both melodramatic cheesy superficial drama and absolutely brutal and progressive depictions of how shatteringly traumatic life in London could be. I often wondered if he, like a modern reader, thought that the brutal stuff was better - if he was doing to cheesy melodrama simply because he was trying to meet the expectations of readers. But I have no idea. Maybe he didn't think the cheesy stuff was cheesy.
  - He was writing serialized stuff, wasn’t he? That that they later put together into a book?

If I’m remembering this correctly, that might account for some of the cheesiness and melodrama, as you were trying to carry people from episode to episode as it were. 

Or could also just be that he was a writer with strengths who was also prone to cheesiness and melodrama
3. Crooklyn is fantastic, definitely one of Lee’s best. It also has my favourite Spike Lee dolly shot with the glue huffers floating upside down. 

I actually enjoy all of Spike Lee’s 90’s movies, I don’t think he has a straight up bad movie until She Hates Me in 2004, which even despite it being awful has some interesting components
4. Is another Spike Lee movie where he inexplicably has music playing over dialogue scenes with a million jump cuts?

More text more text more text more text more text more text more text

## 2. Singin’ in the Rain (1952)

**Score:** 17
**Body:** I recently rewatched this movie as part of the Movie Challenge to watch all 100 movies on AFI’s list: 100 Years 100 Movies. Once again, I genuinely loved this movie. From the excellent performances by the actors to the fun lighthearted story to the excellent production, this movie is all around a great movie. And always a fun watch.

Since the first time I watched this movie years ago, I have become an armchair film history buff. As a result, I am much more familiar with Hollywood history. This time, I picked up on the underlying satire about the movie industry that is in this film. An extra layer is added to this movie when you understand it discusses the challenges that the introduction of sound created for Hollywood. It gives me a lot of empathy for the people in the movie industry and what they were going through.

I love movies that appear to have a simple storyline on the first watch, but have more layers the more you watch it.

The third layer in this movie that I am fascinated by is the references to specific famous people in the movie industry in 1929. This brings up a question for anyone who might know. Can anyone help me identify which famous people are being referenced in this movie? I already identified that the “it” girl is supposed to be Clara Bow. I’d appreciate any help I can get identifying the rest.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's such a great movie, and I too really love the history angle and the depiction of the transition to sound. One reference to a famous person (though not an actor) is the demonstration of the talking picture they play at the party. ("Note how my lips and the sound issuing from them are synchronized together ...") is a reference to Will Hays, who was chairman of the Motion Picture Producers and Distributors Association, and ushered in the Production Code to censor content in Hollywood film. Hays recorded a "talkie" demonstration of the Vitaphone that was played to audiences before *Don Juan.* (You can see that demonstration here if you're interested: [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6pxQCktFC0k](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6pxQCktFC0k) .) 

If you haven't yet seen it, I recommend following up *Singing in the Rain* with *Sunset Boulevard.* That movie, too, is an all-time classic, and it also concerns the transition to sound (though from a different perspective and with a very different tone/attitude towards the transition).
  - Thanks. This sounds familiar. I’m definitely familiar with Will Hays. I also think the lady in the beginning who is announcing everyone on the red carpet was supposed to be Louella Parsons and the movie premiere everyone laughed at… that happened to John Gilbert. I’m surprised this movie didn’t have issues with censors. Maybe it’s one of those things where if celebrities tried to sue they’d have to admit which stories were about them.
2. The other thing I love about SiTR is Jean Hagan's absolutely phenomenal performance as Lina Lamont. She's so good, she's having so much fun, and as a fun in-joke, when Kathy overdubs her you're actually hearing Jean Hagan's real voice, not Debbie Reynolds! What a performance.

It's also a great example of the director Stanley Donen's arc as an artist. This guy did MGM musicals like Singin in the Rain and On the Town, he was just this dependable invisible musical director. He also did the iconic Fred Astaire movie "Royal Wedding" (where [astaire dances on the walls and the ceiling](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=t6KqFK2E7zE&list=RDt6KqFK2E7zE&start_radio=1) in a legendary filmmaking illusion), and then had this really remarkable 2nd half of his career where he became this really lovely and interesting auteur, clearly influenced by the French New Wave but still keeping some of his MGM magic, with movies like "Funny Face," "Charade," and my personal favorite of his, "Two For The Road," which is a movie unlike any other, and probably the single best movie about what it means to be in a marriage. To think that the guy who directed "Two For The Road" and "Singin in the Rain" is the same guy is absolutely incredible to me. What a career.

Also his lifetime achievement Oscar speech is one of the best Oscar speeches ever. It starts around 2 minutes in: [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JMC8kHycgwM](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JMC8kHycgwM)
3. Everyone focuses on Gene Kelly in this movie, and sure, he's a great dancer. But for my money, Donald O'Connor steals the show. He's a match for Kelly, and his Vaudeville schtick in "Make 'Em Laugh" cracks me up every time.


So many people won't watch older movies because they think the older movies won't be relatable, and this movie is such an antidote to that idea. It's a great watch. 
4. It's a great movie (I like it a lot more than the reviewer in the link I'm about to provide!), but worth noting that its portrayal of the "silent era" and the idea that there was a rash of actors struggling to transition to talkies wasn't really true: https://moviessilently.com/2015/10/02/questions-from-the-google-who-were-the-silent-stars-who-failed-in-the-talkies/

## 3. 2025 selections to the National Film Registry thoughts

**Score:** 17
**Body:** Well, It’s that time of year again, Here are my thoughts on the 2025 selection to the National Film Registry

**Before Sunrise** (1995)- Excellent Pick, and I hope Linklater’s other 2 film in the series get put in too in the future

**Brooklyn Bridge** (1981)- Honestly, I never heard of this Documentary, but I see it’s directed by Ken Burns and it's about the hIstory of the Brooklyn Bridge

**Clueless** (1995)- Intriguing Pick, thought I must admit, I honestly thought this was in the National Film Registry already

**Frida** (2002)- I haven’t seen this film, but I hear its great

**Glory** (1989)- I do like this film and I think this is one of Denzel’s best performance, so I’m glad this is in the Registry

**High Society** (1956)- Nice Pick, and it’s probably notable for being Grace Kelly’s last film before she married the Prince Of Monaco

**Inception** (2010)- Hmm, interesting pick, I like this film, but I honestly expect this one to be in the Registry in the Future

**Philadelphia** (1993)- I haven’t seen this one, but I know it’s great, and I plan on seeing this film soon

**Say Amen, Somebody** (1982)- I haven’t seen this one, but I see its a documentary about the history and significance of Gospel Music

**Sparrows** (1926)- Haven’t seen this one

**Ten Nights in a Barroom** (1926)- Haven’t seen this one

**The Big Chill** (1983)- Hmm, Interesting Pick, though I would have pick something else, and with Lawrence Kasdan, I would have picked Body Heat instead

**The Grand Budapest Hotel** (2014), I must admit, I have not seen Grand Budapest Hotel, but I hear it’s great, thought If you ask me, I would’ve rather picked The Royal Tenenbaums (which is not on the list) or Bottle Rocket (Which is Wes Anderson’s first film)

**The Hours** (2002)- Hmm, I don’t know, I feel there are other films that are more worthy than this one

**The Incredibles** (2004)- Yes, Excellent Pick

**The Karate Kid** (1984)- I like this film, so I think this is a good choice

**The Lady** (1925)- I haven’t seen this one

**The Loving Story** (2011)- I haven’t seen this one

**The Maids of McMillain** (1916)- I haven’t seen this one

**The Oath Of The Sword** (1914)- I haven’t seen this one

**The Thing** (1982)- Excellent Pick, one of John Carpenter’s best

**The Tramp & The Dog** (1896)- Haven’t seen this one, but I see this dates back to the 19th Century.

**The Truman Show** (1998)- Excellent Pick, One of Jim Carrey’s best, One of Peter Weir’s best, and I honestly just love this film

**White Christmas** (1954)- I honestly thought this was already in the Registry, so I’m glad this is finally in it

**The Wrecking Crew** (2008)- I haven’t seen this one

So, Overall, this is a very great list of films that the National Film Registry has selected, there are a few selections that I probably would you chosen something else over and some I thought were on the list, but I honestly think this is a great list

All in All, What are your thoughts on these selections?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "I haven't seen movie A but I would've rather picked movie B or C" makes zero sense.

How can you suggest other Wes Anderson movies if you haven't seen the one that is added?




And some characters for the bot
  - Honestly this "opinion post" is some of the most shallow takes I've ever read on this subreddit. "I've heard this movie is good," or "I see this dates back to the 19th Century," what hackneyed analysis is this? I'd prefer if OP just picked a handful of the films on the list they've seen and given a take on the films in question. This is info I could infer from a quick glance at their titles and year of release.
2. Grand Budapest is the best Anderson film, certainly better than Tenenbaums. By far his most ambitious and it hits pretty much every note perfectly. Also his best use of fancy framing devices
3. I think it's important to note that this list isn't just about how good a movie is but how much that movie influences pop culture and other film makers. The Karate Kid is a pretty basic teen sports movies that is really only elevated by a performance from Pat Morita. When you take of the rose tinted goggles of nostaliga it because a pretty good but not particularly great movie. However, it's influence on pop culture is undeniable. It's full of quotes and scenes that people who have not seen the movie can reference.
  - They also select films of cultural significance that have nothing to do with cinema or pop culture like a family’s home movies from the 1940s or a newsreel of a historical event.
4. Truman Show is a fantastic pick and more timely than ever. 

Grand Budapest Hotel is actually more timely than you might imagine as well if you haven't seen it yet. I'd also call it one of Wes Anderson's most accessible films while still being emblematic of the signature highly constructed style that he's known for now. The film is full of great performances, but most of them are more or less in line with Wes Anderson's intentional artifice (don't mean that as a bad thing, it just is what it is) while Ralph Fiennes brings some very welcome naturalism that I think will help wider audiences connect with what they're seeing. He's warm and hilarious and delivers a performance that transcends the usual Wes Anderson style of comedy, while also somehow managing to bring just enough structure so as not to feel out of place among all of the other characters/performances. Meanwhile the story is warm, empathetic, empowering, and antifascist. It's just a really solidly executed film all around.
5. I never will understand the point of selecting a movie like The Incredibles, which I love, for this list. Is The Incredibles in danger of not being preserved? Of course not, it just helps the publicity element which makes the whole thing hollow
  - It isn't in danger now, but 100 years from now? Will Disney still exist as a company to offer it digitally? Will individuals still be archiving and sharing it? (I seed a number of 100+ year old silent films that are hard-ish to find, there are 3-4 people keeping those downloads alive)


What is the future of physical media? Blu-ray are notoriously fragile. Its trending among gen z, but they have nostalgia for their parents CD and DVD collections. What happens when future generations are raised in a digital-only environment.


Will people even still care about film? Maybe they will be plugging into VR experiences so intense and realistic they make today's films look as antiquated as early film feels to us. How many children today would rather plug into Roblox than watch a 20-year old superhero movie?


It's very likely that in 2126 the only people that still care about preserving films like that are people that are in to the history of film and pop culture. Having well-funded preservation programs are really the only way to ensure that our cultural legacy is archived for future generations of researchers and enthusiasts.
6. I can't believe Philadelphia wasn't already in there. Once of my favorite Tom Hanks movies, and Denzel is excellent as well. And Antonio Banderas. I love that movie. I've probably watched it ten times. I used to watch it over and over just for the acting. Also an excellent performance by Jason Robards. I still think about him listening to his baseball games while eating his lunch.
7. A lot of contemporary picks, which is fine, but no movies from the 30's, 40's, 60's nor 70's. Some very interesting picks but also some questionable ones, like High Society.

It's always nice to see newer films such as the Truman Show and Inception being added, but I would love to have more classic ones featured as well.

## 4. The Belly of an Architect (1987) - visually stunning and open to analysis (spoilers)

**Score:** 9
**Body:** I can't remember how this film got recommended to be but I'm guessing it was on some list of visually stunning movies. A lot of it has immaculate composition and lighting that makes the shots resemble paintings. It doesn't have the fancy only-lit-by-candles-and-shot-with-NASA-lenses clout of Barry Lyndon but I feel like it's still on par. Also, it helps when you're shooting beautiful architecture in Rome, I guess.

The story is intriguing as well. It's about Stourley Kracklite, a somewhat over-weight architect from Chicago who is obsessed with his idol, 18th century French architect Boullée. Kracklite and his much younger wife arrive in Rome on Kracklite's birthday. They will be staying in Rome because Kracklite is organizing an exhibition on Boullée along with his benefactor's son Caspasian Speckler. Kracklite develops stomach aches and at first he suspects his wife is poisoning him with figs, which as the movie notes are an aphrodisiac. Meanwhile, his wife quickly starts an affair with Speckler which they don't even bother to hide. Speckler becomes attracted to her because he notices her belly got larger. She in fact became pregnant after having sex with Kracklite on the train on their way to Italy. Speckler is obsessed with her growing pregnant belly. However, as Kracklite is worrying about his stomach pains and the exhibition, he doesn't spend any time with his wife and doesn't notice she's pregnant until she tells him. As Kracklite's stomach issues continue during the months they are in Rome, he himself becomes obsessed with bellies. He photographs the stomachs of ancient male statues, enlarges then with a photocopier and stares at the photos. He also starts writing postcards to Boullée, leading a one-sided conversation with the long-dead architect about his various suspicions and issues. The preparations for the exhibition keep hitting obstacles (including Speckler embezzling some of the funds) and it looks like it might start late, which is unacceptable to Kracklite, because he insists that the exhibition has to open on Boullée's birthday. Eventually, as his health issues become obvious and his behavior erratic, he's kicked off the exhibition committee and Speckler is put in charge. His wife announces to Kracklite that she's leaving him and that she'll be staying with Speckler at least until the child is born. Kracklite finally finds out that he has terminal stomach cancer and not too long to live. In the end, the exhibition does manage to open on Boullée's birthday. Kracklite doesn't participate in the opening ceremony, so Speckler has the very pregnant wife do the ribbon cutting. Kracklite watches over the ceremony in secret. As she cuts the ribbon, the wife goes into labor and Kracklite jumps out of a window and kills himself. 

There are a number of parallels, symbolism and foreshadowing to observe in the movie.

Kracklite's idol Boullée designed many grand buildings that never got built, including a mausoleum for Isaac Newton that would have featured an insanely large dome. Kracklite himself is also obsessed with domes - his wife mentions he built a house for them that was inspired by Boullée and has a dome. Kracklite is given a birthday cake with a dome at the start of the movie and models of buildings with domes are seen throughout the movie. The half-spherical dome of course resembles a belly - a pregnant belly. Kracklite always wanted a child and he's obsessed with pregnant-belly-esque domes, yet he fails to notice his wife's pregnancy when she starts showing. When he sees artsy nude photos she had taken of her pregnant body, he calls them obscene (or even grotesque, iirc), which is ironic considering at that point in her pregnancy she very much resembled a Boullée dome.

The person who immediately notices the wife's pregnancy and is obsessed with pregnant women's bodies is Speckler, the co-organizer of the exhibition. Ironically, he doesn't even care about Boullée  that much - he and others working on the exhibition don't seem to be the least bit enthusiastic about Boullée. So Speckler doesn't care for architectural domes but he does care very much for the bodily domes of pregnant women.

The bellies Kracklite *is* actually interested in are his own and the chiseled stomachs of ancient statues. He first becomes obsessed with a statue of Augustus and with Augustus himself.  At first he's convinced he's being poisoned by his wife using figs, just like Augustus is speculated to have been poisoned by his wife Livia. After he hears about the symptoms of poisoning, he takes a postcard photo of Augustus' statue and enlarges it to make the belly life-size. He compares it with his own belly and becomes obsessed with the spot that is supposed to hurt from poisoning. This develops into a larger obsession with all sorts of statues and their stomach, which he constantly takes photos of, enlarges them and studies them. Ironically, not only are these all flat stomachs (not dome-like at all), but once again his attention is pointed at the wrong stomach - not his wife's, but that of long-dead men.

More could probably be read into the fact that he thinks he's being poisoned by his wife using figs, which are supposed to be an aphrodisiac. In that scenario, would she be trying to make him horny so that she gets noticed by him? Or is she trying to kill him via his libido?

As for the pregnancy, it's interesting that the child was conceived on Kracklite's birthday and born on the day Kracklite died, which is also the birthday of Kracklite's idol Boullée and the date when the exhibition opened. It's as if Kracklite had two children - the real one he didn't care enough about to notice a pregnancy, and the exhibition, which he arguably cared about too much. And he lost both to the same person. Speckler took over Kracklite's "child" the exhibition, also took over Kracklite's wife and will apparently take over duties as the actual child's father. 

The movie is about Kracklite organizing an exhibition on Boullée while dying of stomach cancer. But nobody else in the movie beside him really cares about Boullée or his health issues. While he's obsessive, self-involved and self-aggrandizing, other people don't share his views, wants, cares or needs. Kracklite could be considered a tragic protagonist, except he's really not a great person to be around, certainly not from the point of view of his wife, or even Speckler. The two of them are only antagonists from Kracklite's point of view. 

There are other aspects of the movie I left out here, including Speckler's sister and Kracklite's brief affair with her. There are also many ruminations on death that are noteworthy. It's a really rich movie and I highly recommend it for the visuals, the score, and the intrigue one can analyze afterwards.

## 5. Movies that would be better if the ending happened in the middle

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I was watching Together yesterday, and I liked it well enough. But I couldn’t shake the feeling that the movie might actually be better if its ending happened halfway through.

Spoilers ahead.

The film ends with the fused version of Franco and Brie answering the door for their parents. And my immediate reaction was: Wait, now I want to see what happens. What is this fused version, really? How do they function in the world? How do people react to them? What does their day-to-day look like now?

Instead, the movie ends

I enjoyed it, but it felt like it didn’t quite have enough ideas to justify the full runtime. It starts to spin its wheels, and that’s what made me think it could’ve been far more surprising, and interesting, if the fusion happened closer to the middle of the film. Let the last act actually explore the consequences.

The only other time I’ve felt this way was watching The Fountain. I remember thinking, during the final moments when the timelines finally intersect, that the movie might’ve been even stronger if that reveal happened earlier. If you establish that the timelines can intersect midway through, then the entire third act could be about watching past, present, and future actively collide and reshape each other.

Anyway… can you think of any other films that might actually improve if their ending was repositioned as the midpoint? And do you agree that Together and The Fountain might’ve been more compelling with these changes?

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The most recent Ethan Coen film, "Honey, Don't," actually felt like they ran out of time/money to finish the film or lost a 1/3 of the script and just said "fuck it."


Also, one of Abel Ferrara's 90's movies, a William Gibson inspired, Willem Dafoe and Christopher Walken starring cyber punk drama called New Rose Hotel, was rumored to have actually lost its funding halfway, so the movie just ends with ten minutes of flashbacks. Probably not true, but a funny idea.
2. Glass.

As the follow-up of the highly creative 'Unbreakable' and 'Split', and all the teasing they did around the characters, I was expecting a more smart and creative ending to the trilogy. Moving the main confrontation to the middle would have led to a better resolution than the no-event we got.
  - That is exactly the kind of answer I was hoping for and I agree
